guides.title: How to Get AccelByte API Client Instance
guides.overview: There are two main approaches to obtain an AccelByte API client instance in Unreal Engine. The recommended approach is using the AccelByte OnlineSubsystem (OSS), but you can also create and manage instances manually.

methods.name: Via AccelByte OSS (Recommended)
methods.when_to_use: When using AccelByte OnlineSubsystem plugin for game client. This is the recommended approach for most game implementations.
prerequisites: Player must be logged in via OSS
steps: 6. Access specific APIs via GetXxxApi().Pin() methods

methods.name: Via AccelByte OSS with Custom ID
methods.when_to_use: When using SDK without OSS plugin, or need custom ApiClient identifier
steps: 4. Ensure proper lifetime management of AccelByteInstancePtr

methods.name: Create Manual AccelByte Instance
methods.when_to_use: When not using OSS plugin at all - complete manual management
steps: 3. Store AccelByteInstancePtr to manage its lifetime
4. Get ApiClient from the instance as needed

best_practices: Always validate ApiClient with IsValid() before use
Use TWeakPtr when storing API references (e.g., auto UserApi = ApiClient->GetUserApi().Pin())
Prefer OSS approach for game client implementations
Ensure proper lifetime management of AccelByteInstance when creating manually
The singleton pattern (FRegistry/FMultiRegistry) is deprecated - do not use
For game servers, use GetServerApiClient() instead of GetApiClient()
common_pitfalls: Forgetting to check IsValid() on ApiClient - leads to crashes
Using deprecated FRegistry::GetApiClient() instead of instance-based approach
Not managing AccelByteInstance lifetime when creating manually
Forgetting to Pin() weak API pointers before use
Using wrong GetApiClient method for game server (should use GetServerApiClient)

guides.title: How to Add New API Calls to Unreal SDK
guides.overview: Step-by-step guide to add new API endpoints to the AccelByte Unreal SDK. This involves creating models for request/response, declaring API functions, and optionally exposing to Blueprint.

steps.action: Find endpoint in AccelByte API Explorer
steps.details: Navigate to AccelByte API Explorer and locate the endpoint you want to add. Note the HTTP method, URL path, query parameters, request body, and response format.
Create a USTRUCT that matches the API response JSON structure. Use UPROPERTY macros for each field.
steps.example: For ListGlobalStatItems endpoint returning {data: [...], paging: {...}}, create FAccelByteModelsGlobalStatItemPagingSlicedResult with Data and Paging fields

steps.details: Add function declaration with THandler for success callback and FErrorHandler for error callback. Return FAccelByteTaskWPtr for async task management.
steps.example: FAccelByteTaskWPtr ListGlobalStatItems(THandler<FAccelByteModelsGlobalStatItemPagingSlicedResult> const& OnSuccess, FErrorHandler const& OnError, TArray<FString> const& StatCodes = {}, int32 Limit = 20, int32 Offset = 0);

steps.details: Build the URL using server URL from settings, construct query parameters, and call HttpClient.ApiRequest with appropriate HTTP method.
steps.example: See AccelByteStatisticApi.cpp implementation of ListGlobalStatItems for complete example

steps.details: Create UFUNCTION wrappers with dynamic delegates. Note: Blueprint doesn't support function overloading, so create separate functions for different parameter sets.

best_practices: Always use FReport::Log(FString(__FUNCTION__)) at the start of API function implementations
Validate all parameters before making HTTP calls
Use FAccelByteTaskWPtr return type for async operations
Follow existing naming conventions exactly (AccelByte<Service>Api, FAccelByteModels<Name>)
For Blueprint: Create separate functions for different parameter sets (no overloading)
Use THandler and FErrorHandler for callbacks consistently
Check API pointer validity with IsValid() before calling methods
Use Pin() when accessing weak API pointers
common_pitfalls: Incorrect URL formatting (check server URL setting name)
Not handling optional parameters in query params properly
Forgetting ACCELBYTEUE4SDK_API macro in model structs
Using function overloading in Blueprint wrappers (not supported)

guides.title: How to Authenticate Users with AccelByte
guides.overview: AccelByte supports multiple authentication methods including device ID (guest), email/password, and platform accounts (Steam, Epic, etc.). The OSS identity interface is used post-login; for pre-login API calls (e.g. inside a login widget), use FAccelByteInstance::GetApiClient() instead.

methods.name: Get API Client Before Login (Pre-login Access)
methods.when_to_use: Any time you need to call AccelByte APIs before login is complete — for example, inside a login widget that calls LoginWithDeviceId or LoginWithOtherPlatform. Works for both SDK and OSS modes. GetApiClient(int32) returns null before login; use this pattern instead.
steps: 3. Call Instance->GetApiClient() with no args — returns the 'default' keyed client, auto-creating it if needed from SDK settings

methods.name: Device ID Login (Guest Account) — OSS style
methods.when_to_use: Post-login slot-based access via OSS identity interface. Use only after a player is already registered in an OSS slot.
steps: 3. Leave Id and Token empty for device ID login
5. Bind to OnLoginComplete delegate to handle result

methods.name: Email and Password Login
methods.when_to_use: For permanent user accounts with credentials

best_practices: Always bind OnLoginComplete delegate before calling Login
Check login success status in callback before accessing ApiClient
Store ApiClient after successful login for use by other systems
Use device ID for development/testing, proper auth for production
Implement proper error handling for login failures
common_pitfalls: Using ABSubsystem->GetApiClient(0) inside a login widget — this returns null because slot 0 is only populated after a successful OSS login completes. Use GetAccelByteInstance().Pin()->GetApiClient() for pre-login API access.
Using GetApiClient(int32) anywhere before login — the int32 slot lookup chain (LocalUserNum → UniqueNetId → AccelByteId) is empty until the OSS login task runs RegisterApiClient.

guides.title: How to Implement Matchmaking with AccelByte
guides.overview: AccelByte matchmaking (V2) uses party sessions and match pools. Create a party session first, then start matchmaking to find opponents.
prerequisites: Session templates configured in AccelByte Admin Portal
Match pools configured in session template

steps.action: Configure session templates in Admin Portal
steps.details: Go to Multiplayer > Session Template and create party session template with match pool configuration
Use OnlineSession interface to create a party session with SETTING_SESSION_TYPE_PARTY_SESSION
Call StartMatchmaking with match pool settings and search handle
OnStartMatchmakingComplete callback fires when match is found
Call JoinSession with the matched session from search results

best_practices: Always create party session before matchmaking
Configure session templates in Admin Portal first
Use V2 sessions (bEnableV2Sessions=true)
Handle matchmaking cancellation properly
Add delay before joining to allow P2P host initialization
common_pitfalls: Not configuring session templates in Admin Portal first
Forgetting to create party session before matchmaking
Joining session immediately without waiting for P2P host setup
Not handling matchmaking cancellation edge cases

guides.title: How to Query and Display Player Achievements
guides.overview: Query achievement definitions and user progress using the Achievement API

steps.details: Use HTTP module to download images from achievement icon URLs

best_practices: Query definitions once and cache them
Query user achievements when needed to get latest progress
Download and cache achievement icons
Use Pin() when accessing weak Achievement API pointer
